[[別表神社]] |創建 = （伝）第14代[[仲哀天皇]]年間 |本殿 = 八棟造（織田造） |別名 = 織田明神 |札所等 = |例祭 = [[10月9日]] |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Fukui|label=劔神社|coordinates= {{coord2|35|57|28.2|N|136|3|19.4|E|}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-width=200|type=point|frame-latitude=35.96|frame-longitude=136.08}} }} [[File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), otorii.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{center|大鳥居}}]] '''劔神社'''（つるぎじんじゃ）は、[[福井県]][[丹生郡]][[越前町]]織田にある[[神社]]。[[式内社]]、[[越前国]][[一宮|二宮]]。[[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[国幣小社]]で、現在は[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]。別名を「織田明神（おたみょうじん）」とも。 == 祭神 == [[スサノオ|素盞嗚尊]]を主祭神とし、気比大神・[[忍熊皇子|忍熊王]]を配祀する。 == 歴史 == === 創建 === 社伝によれば、御神体となっている剣は[[垂仁天皇]]皇子の[[五十瓊敷入彦命]]が作らせた神剣で、[[神功皇后]]摂政の時代に[[仲哀天皇]]皇子の忍熊王が譲り受け、忍熊王が[[高志国]]（[[越国]]）の賊徒討伐にあたり無事平定した。のち、[[伊部郷]]座ヶ岳に祀られていた素盞嗚尊の神霊を伊部臣が現在地に勧請し、この神剣を[[御霊代]]とし祀ったことに始まると伝えられる。忍熊王はその後もこの地を開拓したことから、開拓の[[祖神]]として父である仲哀天皇（気比大神）とともに配祀されたと伝える。 === 概史 === 古くは社蔵の梵鐘（国宝）に「劔御子寺鐘 神護景雲四年九月十一日」の銘が見え、奈良時代の[[神護景雲]]4年（[[770年]]）当時にはすでに[[神宮寺]]があったことが知られる。 [[六国史|国史]]では、[[宝亀]]2年（[[771年]]）に従四位下勲六等の「劔神」に食封20戸・田2町を与えると見える。また[[天安 (日本)|天安]]3年（[[859年]]）には、「劔神」の[[神階]]を従四位上勲六等から正四位下勲六等に昇叙する旨が記載されている。 『[[新抄格勅符抄]]』[[大同 (日本)|大同]]元年（[[806年]]）牒では、当時の「劔御子神」には[[神戸 (民戸)|神戸]]として[[越前国]]から30戸が充てられており、そのうち10戸は[[天平神護]]元年（[[765年]]）、20戸は宝亀3年（[[772年]]）に定められたとする。 [[延長 (元号)|延長]]5年（[[927年]]）成立の『[[延喜式]]』[[延喜式神名帳|神名帳]]では、越前国[[敦賀郡]]に「劔神社」と記載され、[[式内社]]に列している（ただし[[敦賀市]]莇生野の[[劒神社 (敦賀市莇生野)|劔神社]]に比定する説もある）。 中世以降、この地の歴代の領主、[[斯波氏]]、[[朝倉氏]]、[[織田氏]]の崇敬を受け保護された。特に[[織田信長]]は当社の[[神職|神官]]の子孫とされ{{Cite web|和書|title=戦国を訪ねる旅マップ（福井・滋賀・京都三府県連携観光促進協議会）|work=|date=|url=https://www.pref.shiga.lg.jp/file/attachment/5221060.pdf|accessdate=2023-08-21|publisher=滋賀県}}[https://web.archive.org/web/20141214065127/http://zasshi.news.yahoo.co.jp/article?a=20141116-00000001-sasahi-peo 2014年11月16日Yahoo!ニュース 雑誌関連記事「織田家第18代当主　織田家の400年の伝統を破り、息子の名に「信」をつけず」]「週刊朝日」2014年11月21日号 リンク切れIA差し替え 2017年5月7日閲覧 、[[織田家]]の[[氏神]]とされた神田千里『織田信長』P.26 2014年 ちくま新書。[[柴田勝家]]の書状にも「殿様、御氏神、粗相仕るべからず」とある。尾張織田家はもともと越前織田荘出身で姓とし、主君の斯波氏の尾張守護任官で就いて尾張へ移ったので、数代前の越前守護[[斯波義将]]の被官の[[藤原信昌|織田信昌]]、将広、父子も明徳4年（1393年）6月7日に「修理再興し、今後も課役を免除し、子々孫々修理を怠らない」と誓約しており、当社は織田氏と深い関係がある『織田家の人々』小和田哲男 p.12 1991年 河出書房新社、『天下人の父・織田信秀 信長は何を学び受け継いだのか』谷口克広 pp.22-23 2017年 祥伝社新書<史料は「織田劔神社文書」所収「仮名置文」>。江戸時代には[[福井藩]]・[[大野藩]]両藩からの崇敬を受け、幕末には[[伏見宮家]]の[[祈願所]]ともされた。昭和3年に[[国幣小社]]に列格した。第二次大戦後は[[神社本庁]]の[[別表神社]]となった。 === 神階 === * [[宝亀]]2年（[[771年]]）10月16日時点、従四位下勲六等（『[[続日本紀]]』） - 表記は「劔神」。 * [[天安 (日本)|天安]]3年（[[859年]]）1月27日、従四位上勲六等から正四位下勲六等（『[[日本三代実録]]』） - 表記は「劔神」。 == 境内 == File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), honden.jpg|本殿（福井県指定文化財） File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from an angle.jpg|拝殿 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), gomadou.jpg|神輿庫（旧神前院護摩堂、越前町指定文化財） File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), Ota-jinja.jpg|摂社織田神社（福井県指定文化財） File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-2.jpg|旧神宮寺礎石
{{small|塔心礎か。}} File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), torii.jpg|境内入り口 == 文化財 == === 国宝 === * 梵鐘 銘劔御子寺鐘神護景雲四年九月十一日（工芸品） *: 奈良時代の作。「劔御子寺鐘/神護景雲四/年九月十一日」（神護景雲4年は[[770年]]）の銘がある[[梵鐘]]で、奈良時代の在銘鐘として貴重なものである。[[道鏡]]が失脚した際に[[神馬]]とともに奉納されたとの伝承がある。越前町織田文化歴史館に寄託。1902年（明治35年）4月17日に当時の[[古社寺保存法]]に基づき国宝に指定、1956年（昭和31年）6月28日に[[文化財保護法]]に基づき国宝に指定{{国指定文化財等データベース|201|479|梵鐘}}{{Cite web|和書|author= |year= |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/125674 |title=文化遺産データベース「梵鐘」 |publisher=文化庁 |accessdate=2015-12-02}}。 === 重要文化財 === [[File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja Echizen).jpg|thumb|220px|{{center|八相涅槃図}}]] * 絹本著色八相涅槃図（附 紙本墨書涅槃講式（断簡）1巻）（絵画） *: 鎌倉時代中期の作。[[奈良国立博物館]]に寄託。1914年（大正3年）4月17日指定{{Cite web|和書|author= |year= |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/150731 |title=文化遺産データベース「絹本著色八相涅槃図」 |publisher=文化庁 |accessdate=2015-12-02}}。 === 福井県指定文化財 === * 有形文化財 ** 本殿（建造物） - 江戸時代の造営。1979年（昭和54年）2月6日指定[http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/kenzou/tsurugijinja-honden.html 劔神社本殿]（福井県ホームページ「福井の文化財」）。。 ** 摂社織田神社本殿（建造物） - 室町時代の造営。1971年（昭和46年）4月16日指定[http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/kenzou/tsurugijinja-oitajinja.html 劔神社の摂社織田神社]（福井県ホームページ「福井の文化財」）。。 ** 不動明王三尊像 3幅（絵画） - 南北朝時代の作。1984年（昭和59年）3月2日指定[http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/kaiga/tsurugijinja-fudomyoo.html 不動明王三尊像]（福井県ホームページ「福井の文化財」）。。 ** 密教法具金銅盤（工芸品） - 平安時代の作。1971年（昭和46年）4月16日指定[http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/kougei/tsurugijinja-mikkyohogu.html 密教法具金銅盤]（福井県ホームページ「福井の文化財」）。。 ** 劔神社文書（古文書） - 室町時代から明治時代の古文書210点。2013年（平成25年）3月22日指定[http://info.pref.fukui.jp/bunka/bunkazai/sitei/shoseki/tsurugijinzya-monzyo.html 劔神社文書]（福井県ホームページ「福井の文化財」）。。 === 越前町指定文化財 === * 有形文化財 ** 旧神前院護摩堂（建造物） - 江戸時代の造営。1987年（昭和62年）11月1日指定。 ** 神輿 1対（工芸品） - 江戸時代中期の作。1978年（昭和53年）2月17日指定。 ** 獅子頭（工芸品） - 江戸時代初期の作。1978年（昭和53年）2月17日指定。 === その他 === *織田信長書状 *徳川家康書状 *柴田勝家書状 *古文書など == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|2}} == 関連図書 == * 安津素彦・梅田義彦編集兼監修者『神道辞典』[[神社新報]]社、1968年、40頁 * [[白井永二]]・土岐昌訓編集『神社辞典』[[東京堂出版]]、1979年、237頁 == 関連項目 == * [[織田荘 (越前国)|織田荘]] == 外部リンク == {{Commonscat|Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui)}} * [http://tsurugi-jinja.jp// 劔神社] - 公式サイト * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/300201.html 剣神社] - 國學院大學21世紀COEプログラム「神道・神社史料集成」 {{shinto-stub}} {{神道 横}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:つるきしんしや}} [[Category:福井県の神社]] [[Category:越前町の歴史]] [[Category:越前町の建築物]] [[Category:越前国の式内社]] [[Category:越前国|社2]] [[Category:二宮]] [[Category:別表神社]] [[Category:国幣小社]] [[Category:日本の国宝 (工芸品)]] [[Category:福井県の国宝]] [[Category:福井県の重要文化財]] [[Category:福井県指定有形文化財]] [[Category:福井県内の市町村指定有形文化財]] [[Category:織田氏|社つるきしんしや]] [[Category:朝倉氏|社つるきしんしや]] [[Category:斯波氏|社つるきしんしや]] [[Category:福井藩]] [[Category:大野藩]]

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building|image=Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from afar.jpg}} '''Tsurugi Shrine''', also known as Oda Myojin, is a Shinto shrine located in Oda, [[Echizen, Fukui|Echizen]] Town, [[Nyu]] District, [[Fukui Prefecture]]. It is classified as a Shinto shrine in the Engishiki and is one of the two main shrines in [[Echizen Province]]. Its former rank was a Kokuhei Shōsha (national shrine of lower rank), and it is now listed as a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
It is the [[Ninomiya]] of [[Echizen Province]]
== Other websites == * [http://tsurugi-jinja.jp// Tsurugi Shrine] - Official Site * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/300201.html Sword Shrine] - Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program "Shinto Shrine Historical Materials Collection"
[[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Infobox mapframe without OSM relation ID on Wikidata]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Fukui Prefecture]]

{{infobox religious building|image=Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from afar.jpg}} '''Tsurugi Shrine''', also known as Oda Myojin, is a Shinto shrine located in Oda, [[Echizen, Fukui|Echizen]] Town, [[Nyu]] District, [[Fukui Prefecture]]. It is classified as a Shinto shrine in the Engishiki and is one of the two main shrines in [[Echizen Province]]. Its former rank was a Kokuhei Shōsha (national shrine of lower rank), and it is now listed as a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
It is the [[Ninomiya]] of [[Echizen Province]]
== Other websites == * [http://tsurugi-jinja.jp// Tsurugi Shrine] - Official Site * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/300201.html Sword Shrine] - Kokugakuin University 21st Century COE Program "Shinto Shrine Historical Materials Collection"
[[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Infobox mapframe without OSM relation ID on Wikidata]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Original pages]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]][[fa:معبد تسوروگی]][[ja:劔神社]][[fr:Tsurugi-jinja]]

{{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishikiirihiko|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja Echizen).jpg|alt=八相成道図|Eight-phase Nirvana File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), gomadou.jpg|alt=護摩堂|Gomadou (Fire Ritual Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden closeup.jpg|alt=拝殿 クローズアップ|Haiden closeup (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from afar.jpg|alt=拝殿 遠景|Haiden from afar (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from an angle.jpg|alt=拝殿 角度から|Haiden from an angle (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), honden.jpg|alt=本殿|Honden (Main Shrine) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), Ota-jinja.jpg|alt=太田神社|Ota-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), otorii.jpg|alt=大鳥居|Otorii (Grand Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-1.jpg|alt=礎石-1|Foundation Stone 1 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-2.jpg|alt=礎石-2|Foundation Stone 2 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), torii.jpg|alt=鳥居|Torii (Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706993601).jpg|alt=壱景|A view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706995651).jpg|alt=別の景色|Another view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugijinja-haiden01.jpg|alt=拝殿|Haiden (Worship Hall) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] {{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishikiirihiko|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja Echizen).jpg|alt=八相成道図|Eight-phase Nirvana File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), gomadou.jpg|alt=護摩堂|Gomadou (Fire Ritual Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden closeup.jpg|alt=拝殿 クローズアップ|Haiden closeup (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from afar.jpg|alt=拝殿 遠景|Haiden from afar (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from an angle.jpg|alt=拝殿 角度から|Haiden from an angle (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), honden.jpg|alt=本殿|Honden (Main Shrine) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), Ota-jinja.jpg|alt=太田神社|Ota-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), otorii.jpg|alt=大鳥居|Otorii (Grand Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-1.jpg|alt=礎石-1|Foundation Stone 1 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-2.jpg|alt=礎石-2|Foundation Stone 2 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), torii.jpg|alt=鳥居|Torii (Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706993601).jpg|alt=壱景|A view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706995651).jpg|alt=別の景色|Another view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugijinja-haiden01.jpg|alt=拝殿|Haiden (Worship Hall) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:Tsurugi Shrine]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]] [[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Fukui Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Susanoo-no-Mikoto shrines]] [[Category:Draft]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Echizen Province]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:National Treasure of Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Draft articles]] [[Category:WikipWikipedia draftsedia drafts]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Infobox mapframe without OSM relation ID on Wikidata]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:Tsurugi Shrine]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]] [[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Draft articles]][[fr:User:{{PAGENAME}}]][[simple:User:{{PAGENAME}}]] }} [[en:Draft:Tsurugi Shrine]]

{{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishikiirihiko|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja Echizen).jpg|alt=八相成道図|Eight-phase Nirvana File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), gomadou.jpg|alt=護摩堂|Gomadou (Fire Ritual Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden closeup.jpg|alt=拝殿 クローズアップ|Haiden closeup (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from afar.jpg|alt=拝殿 遠景|Haiden from afar (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from an angle.jpg|alt=拝殿 角度から|Haiden from an angle (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), honden.jpg|alt=本殿|Honden (Main Shrine) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), Ota-jinja.jpg|alt=太田神社|Ota-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), otorii.jpg|alt=大鳥居|Otorii (Grand Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-1.jpg|alt=礎石-1|Foundation Stone 1 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-2.jpg|alt=礎石-2|Foundation Stone 2 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), torii.jpg|alt=鳥居|Torii (Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706993601).jpg|alt=壱景|A view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706995651).jpg|alt=別の景色|Another view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugijinja-haiden01.jpg|alt=拝殿|Haiden (Worship Hall) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishikiirihiko|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja Echizen).jpg|alt=八相成道図|Eight-phase Nirvana File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), gomadou.jpg|alt=護摩堂|Gomadou (Fire Ritual Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden closeup.jpg|alt=拝殿 クローズアップ|Haiden closeup (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from afar.jpg|alt=拝殿 遠景|Haiden from afar (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from an angle.jpg|alt=拝殿 角度から|Haiden from an angle (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), honden.jpg|alt=本殿|Honden (Main Shrine) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), Ota-jinja.jpg|alt=太田神社|Ota-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), otorii.jpg|alt=大鳥居|Otorii (Grand Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-1.jpg|alt=礎石-1|Foundation Stone 1 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-2.jpg|alt=礎石-2|Foundation Stone 2 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), torii.jpg|alt=鳥居|Torii (Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706993601).jpg|alt=壱景|A view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706995651).jpg|alt=別の景色|Another view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugijinja-haiden01.jpg|alt=拝殿|Haiden (Worship Hall) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishikiirihiko|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja Echizen).jpg|alt=八相成道図|Eight-phase Nirvana File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), gomadou.jpg|alt=護摩堂|Gomadou (Fire Ritual Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden closeup.jpg|alt=拝殿 クローズアップ|Haiden closeup (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from afar.jpg|alt=拝殿 遠景|Haiden from afar (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from an angle.jpg|alt=拝殿 角度から|Haiden from an angle (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), honden.jpg|alt=本殿|Honden (Main Shrine) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), Ota-jinja.jpg|alt=太田神社|Ota-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), otorii.jpg|alt=大鳥居|Otorii (Grand Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-1.jpg|alt=礎石-1|Foundation Stone 1 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-2.jpg|alt=礎石-2|Foundation Stone 2 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), torii.jpg|alt=鳥居|Torii (Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706993601).jpg|alt=壱景|A view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706995651).jpg|alt=別の景色|Another view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugijinja-haiden01.jpg|alt=拝殿|Haiden (Worship Hall) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] {{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishikiirihiko|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja {{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja revision.text:
{{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja Echizen).jpg|alt=八相成道図|Eight-phase Nirvana File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), gomadou.jpg|alt=護摩堂|Gomadou (Fire Ritual Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden closeup.jpg|alt=拝殿 クローズアップ|Haiden closeup (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from afar.jpg|alt=拝殿 遠景|Haiden from afar (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from an angle.jpg|alt=拝殿 角度から|Haiden from an angle (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), honden.jpg|alt=本殿|Honden (Main Shrine) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), Ota-jinja.jpg|alt=太田神社|Ota-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), otorii.jpg|alt=大鳥居|Otorii (Grand Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-1.jpg|alt=礎石-1|Foundation Stone 1 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-2.jpg|alt=礎石-2|Foundation Stone 2 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), torii.jpg|alt=鳥居|Torii (Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706993601).jpg|alt=壱景|A view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706995651).jpg|alt=別の景色|Another view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugijinja-haiden01.jpg|alt=拝殿|Haiden (Worship Hall) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] revision.text:
{{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja Echizen).jpg|alt=八相成道図|Eight-phase Nirvana File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), gomadou.jpg|alt=護摩堂|Gomadou (Fire Ritual Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden closeup.jpg|alt=拝殿 クローズアップ|Haiden closeup (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from afar.jpg|alt=拝殿 遠景|Haiden from afar (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from an angle.jpg|alt=拝殿 角度から|Haiden from an angle (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), honden.jpg|alt=本殿|Honden (Main Shrine) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), Ota-jinja.jpg|alt=太田神社|Ota-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), otorii.jpg|alt=大鳥居|Otorii (Grand Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-1.jpg|alt=礎石-1|Foundation Stone 1 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-2.jpg|alt=礎石-2|Foundation Stone 2 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), torii.jpg|alt=鳥居|Torii (Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706993601).jpg|alt=壱景|A view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706995651).jpg|alt=別の景色|Another view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugijinja-haiden01.jpg|alt=拝殿|Haiden (Worship Hall) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Tsurugi Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shosha]] [[Category:Fukui Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Susanoo-no-Mikoto shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:Echizen Province]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:National Treasure of Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Infobox mapframe without OSM relation ID on Wikidata]] [[Category:Coordinates on Wikidata]] [[Category:Kokuhei Shōsha]] [[en:Tsurugi Shrine]][[Category:Ninomiya]] [[Category:Fukui Prefecture]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:unsorted link Shrine]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:Unsorted link categories]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] {{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja Echizen).jpg|alt=八相成道図|Eight-phase Nirvana File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), gomadou.jpg|alt=護摩堂|Gomadou (Fire Ritual Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden closeup.jpg|alt=拝殿 クローズアップ|Haiden closeup (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from afar.jpg|alt=拝殿 遠景|Haiden from afar (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from an angle.jpg|alt=拝殿 角度から|Haiden from an angle (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), honden.jpg|alt=本殿|Honden (Main Shrine) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), Ota-jinja.jpg|alt=太田神社|Ota-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), otorii.jpg|alt=大鳥居|Otorii (Grand Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-1.jpg|alt=礎石-1|Foundation Stone 1 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-2.jpg|alt=礎石-2|Foundation Stone 2 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), torii.jpg|alt=鳥居|Torii (Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706993601).jpg|alt=壱景|A view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706995651).jpg|alt=別の景色|Another view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugijinja-haiden01.jpg|alt=拝殿|Haiden (Worship Hall) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja {{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja Echizen).jpg|alt=八相成道図|Eight-phase Nirvana File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), gomadou.jpg|alt=護摩堂|Gomadou (Fire Ritual Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden closeup.jpg|alt=拝殿 クローズアップ|Haiden
