[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja Echizen).jpg|alt=八相成道図|Eight-phase Nirvana File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), gomadou.jpg|alt=護摩堂|Gomadou (Fire Ritual Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden closeup.jpg|alt=拝殿 クローズアップ|Haiden closeup (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from afar.jpg|alt=拝殿 遠景|Haiden from afar (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from an angle.jpg|alt=拝殿 角度から|Haiden from an angle (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), honden.jpg|alt=本殿|Honden (Main Shrine) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), Ota-jinja.jpg|alt=太田神社|Ota-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), otorii.jpg|alt=大鳥居|Otorii (Grand Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-1.jpg|alt=礎石-1|Foundation Stone 1 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-2.jpg|alt=礎石-2|Foundation Stone 2 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), torii.jpg|alt=鳥居|Torii (Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706993601).jpg|alt=壱景|A view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706995651).jpg|alt=別の景色|Another view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugijinja-haiden01.jpg|alt=拝殿|Haiden (Worship Hall) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] revision.text:
{{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja Echizen).jpg|alt=八相成道図|Eight-phase Nirvana File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), gomadou.jpg|alt=護摩堂|Gomadou (Fire Ritual Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden closeup.jpg|alt=拝殿 クローズアップ|Haiden closeup (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from afar.jpg|alt=拝殿 遠景|Haiden from afar (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from an angle.jpg|alt=拝殿 角度から|Haiden from an angle (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), honden.jpg|alt=本殿|Honden (Main Shrine) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), Ota-jinja.jpg|alt=太田神社|Ota-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), otorii.jpg|alt=大鳥居|Otorii (Grand Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-1.jpg|alt=礎石-1|Foundation Stone 1 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-2.jpg|alt=礎石-2|Foundation Stone 2 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), torii.jpg|alt=鳥居|Torii (Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706993601).jpg|alt=壱景|A view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706995651).jpg|alt=別の景色|Another view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugijinja-haiden01.jpg|alt=拝殿|Haiden (Worship Hall) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] {{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja Echizen).jpg|alt=八相成道図|Eight-phase Nirvana File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), gomadou.jpg|alt=護摩堂|Gomadou (Fire Ritual Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden closeup.jpg|alt=拝殿 クローズアップ|Haiden closeup (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from afar.jpg|alt=拝殿 遠景|Haiden from afar (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from an angle.jpg|alt=拝殿 角度から|Haiden from an angle (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), honden.jpg|alt=本殿|Honden (Main Shrine) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), Ota-jinja.jpg|alt=太田神社|Ota-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), otorii.jpg|alt=大鳥居|Otorii (Grand Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-1.jpg|alt=礎石-1|Foundation Stone 1 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-2.jpg|alt=礎石-2|Foundation Stone 2 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), torii.jpg|alt=鳥居|Torii (Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706993601).jpg|alt=壱景|A view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706995651).jpg|alt=別の景色|Another view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugijinja-haiden01.jpg|alt=拝殿|Haiden (Worship Hall) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] {{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja Echizen).jpg|alt=八相成道図|Eight-phase Nirvana File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), gomadou.jpg|alt=護摩堂|Gomadou (Fire Ritual Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden closeup.jpg|alt=拝殿 クローズアップ|Haiden closeup (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from afar.jpg|alt=拝殿 遠景|Haiden from afar (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from an angle.jpg|alt=拝殿 角度から|Haiden from an angle (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), honden.jpg|alt=本殿|Honden (Main Shrine) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), Ota-jinja.jpg|alt=太田神社|Ota-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), otorii.jpg|alt=大鳥居|Otorii (Grand Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-1.jpg|alt=礎石-1|Foundation Stone 1 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-2.jpg|alt=礎石-2|Foundation Stone 2 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), torii.jpg|alt=鳥居|Torii (Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706993601).jpg|alt=壱景|A view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706995651).jpg|alt=別の景色|Another view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugijinja-haiden01.jpg|alt=拝殿|Haiden (Worship Hall) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] {{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja {{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja {{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja Echizen).jpg|alt=八相成道図|Eight-phase Nirvana File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), gomadou.jpg|alt=護摩堂|Gomadou (Fire Ritual Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden closeup.jpg|alt=拝殿 クローズアップ|Haiden closeup (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from afar.jpg|alt=拝殿 遠景|Haiden from afar (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from an angle.jpg|alt=拝殿 角度から|Haiden from an angle (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), honden.jpg|alt=本殿|Honden (Main Shrine) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), Ota-jinja.jpg|alt=太田神社|Ota-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), otorii.jpg|alt=大鳥居|Otorii (Grand Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-1.jpg|alt=礎石-1|Foundation Stone 1 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-2.jpg|alt=礎石-2|Foundation Stone 2 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), torii.jpg|alt=鳥居|Torii (Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706993601).jpg|alt=壱景|A view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706995651).jpg|alt=別の景色|Another view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugijinja-haiden01.jpg|alt=拝殿|Haiden (Worship Hall) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] {{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[Category:short pages]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja {{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[Category:short pages]][[Category:short pages]][[Category:short pages]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja {{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[Category:short pages]][[Category:short pages]][[Category:short pages]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja [[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja {{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[Category:short pages]][[Category:short pages]][[Category:short pages]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja {{About-distinguish|the shrine in Honshi|Mount Tsurugi (Tokushima)}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}} ---- Merge this content to the proper page [[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] and fix [[simple:User:Immanuelle/Sugōisobe Shrine]] too and[[simple:User:Immanuelle/Oagata Shrine]] ---- {{infobox religious building |name=Tsurugi Shrine| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden-1.jpg | caption = haiden[[fa:معبد_تسوروگی]][[ja:劔神社]][[simple:Tsurugi Shrine]] | map_type = Japan | coordinates = {{coord|35|57|28.22|N|136|3|19.42|E}} | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]],
[[Ōkuninushi|Onamuchi]],
{{ill|Prince Oshikuma|ja|忍熊皇子}} }} {{Expand Japanese|劔神社}}{{complex}} {{ill|Tsurugi Shrine|simple|4=ja|5=劔神社|en}} ([[:ja:劔神社|劔神社]]) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in {{Ill|Ota, Fukui|lt=Ota|en}}, [[Fukui Prefecture]], [[Japan]].{{Cite web |title=Tsurugi Shrine - With a Long History of 1,800 Years, This Shrine in Fukui Prefecture Is a Soothing Power Spot Where You Can Enjoy the Beauty of Nature! A Look at the Highlights of Tsurugi Shrine, Including the Temple Bell, a National Treasure! - Art & Architecture｜COOL JAPAN VIDEOS｜A Website With Information About Travel, Culture, Food, History, and Things to Do in Japan |url=https://cooljapan-videos.com/en/articles/jlvxujs2 |access-date=2023-12-02 |website=cooljapan-videos.com |language=en}}https://web.archive.org/web/20220120150410/https://info.pref.fukui.lg.jp/hyakkei/40_english/010_view/pdf/062.pdf It is a [[Beppyo shrine]], or a shrine that is particularly notable in a certain way with a significant history to it.{{Cite web |title=別表神社とは？御朱印めぐりに参考になる「別表神社一覧」とマップ {{!}} 開運戦隊ゴシュインジャー |url=https://jinja-gosyuin.com/beppyou/ |access-date=2023-04-02 |website=jinja-gosyuin.com}} It is a [[Ichinomiya|Ninomiya]], or a second rank shrine in its province after the [[Ichinomiya]]. == History == === Establishment === According to shrine tradition, the sacred sword that serves as the deity was created by the imperial prince {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命}} son of [[Emperor Suinin]], who gave it to [[Ōkuninushi|Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto]] during the reign of [[Empress Jingū|Empress Jingu]]. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto then successfully subdued bandits in the Koshi Province (Echizen Country). Later, the spirit of Susanoo-no-Mikoto, originally enshrined at Ibegori on Mount Ibuki, was moved to the current location by the Ibeminister, and the sacred sword was enshrined as the spirit's symbol. Oonamuchi-no-Mikoto is also worshipped as an [[Oyagami]] for his contributions to developing the land, alongside his father, Emperor Naka no Ōe (Kehi-no-Kami). === General History === An ancient temple bell (a National Treasure) in the shrine's possession bears the inscription "Tsurugi Mikoji Temple Bell, Jingokyoen 4th Year, 9th Month, 11th Day," revealing that a {{Ill|Jingu-ji|en|4=ja|5=神宮寺|6=fr|7=Jingū-ji}} (a type of Buddhist temple associated with a Shinto shrine) already existed by 770 (the fourth year of the Jingokyoen era) during the [[Nara period]]. In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja Echizen).jpg|alt=八相成道図|Eight-phase Nirvana File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), gomadou.jpg|alt=護摩堂|Gomadou (Fire Ritual Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden closeup.jpg|alt=拝殿 クローズアップ|Haiden closeup (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from afar.jpg|alt=拝殿 遠景|Haiden from afar (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), haiden from an angle.jpg|alt=拝殿 角度から|Haiden from an angle (Worship Hall) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), honden.jpg|alt=本殿|Honden (Main Shrine) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), Ota-jinja.jpg|alt=太田神社|Ota-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), otorii.jpg|alt=大鳥居|Otorii (Grand Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-1.jpg|alt=礎石-1|Foundation Stone 1 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), soseki-2.jpg|alt=礎石-2|Foundation Stone 2 File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen town), torii.jpg|alt=鳥居|Torii (Shrine Gate) File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706993601).jpg|alt=壱景|A view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugi-jinja (Echizen, Fukui) (8706995651).jpg|alt=別の景色|Another view of Tsurugi-jinja File:Tsurugijinja-haiden01.jpg|alt=拝殿|Haiden (Worship Hall) == References == {{reflist}} {{Draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] In national history, the shrine is recorded to have been granted 20 households and 2 cho (units of area) of land with the rank of Junior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, and Sixth Merit in 771 (the second year of the Houki era). Furthermore, in 859 (the third year of the Ten'an era), the shrine's divine rank was elevated from Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade, Sixth Merit to Senior Fourth Rank, Lower Grade, Sixth Merit. The "Shinsen Shoukafu," dated to the first year of the Daido era (806), mentions that 30 households from Echizen Country were allocated to "Tsurugi Mikoshi" at that time, with 10 households assigned in the first year of the Tenpyo-Jingo era (765) and the remaining 20 in the third year of the Houki era (772). In the [[Engishiki]], compiled in 927, the shrine is listed in the Tsuruga District of Echizen Country as "Tsurugi Shrine," marking its status as a Shikinaisha (though some theorize it could also refer to another Tsurugi Shrine in Aonono, Tsuruga City). Throughout the medieval period, the shrine was revered and protected by the successive lords of the region, including the Shibata, Asakura, and Oda clans. Notably, [[Oda Nobunaga]] is said to have descended from priests of the shrine and revered it as the clan's tutelary deity{{Cite web|date=2023-01-18|title=Tsurugi-jinja Shrine|url=https://www.the-kansai-guide.com/en/directory/item/11786/|access-date=2024-03-28|website=The KANSAI Guide - The Origin of Japan, KANSAI|language=en}}. Letters from {{Ill|Shibata Katsuie|en|4=ja|5=柴田勝家}} also indicate the shrine's importance. The {{Ill|Oda clan|en|4=ja|5=織田氏}} of Owari originally hailed from Oda in Echizen and took on their surname when they moved to Owari as guardians under the Shibata clan, signifying a deep connection with the shrine. In the Edo period, the shrine was revered by both the Fukui and Ono domains, and by the end of the period, it had become a prayer site for the Fushimi Imperial family. It was designated a Kokuhei Shosha in 1928 and became a [[Beppyo shrine]] of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] after World War II. ==Gallery== File:Eight-phase Nirvana (Tsurugi Jinja
